title: Rust 1.96.0: Copy Ranges, Pattern Asserts, and Two Cargo CVEs
summary: Rust 1.96.0 shipped May 28 with new Copy-able range types, stabilized pattern-matching assert macros, and a stricter WebAssembly linker default. Two Cargo security advisories — one medium, one low — round out a release that is about language ergonomics and tooling hygiene, not performance.
body:
The Rust team shipped 1.96.0 on May 28, 2026. The changelog leads with language ergonomics and ends with a security advisory that affects anyone running dependencies through a third-party registry. Nothing in between involves performance numbers. We checked.

**Copy Range types (RFC 3550)**

The old `Range` types implement `Iterator` directly. This makes them non-`Copy`. If you want to store a range in a struct that also needs to be `Copy`, you can't — so you split `start` and `end` into separate fields and reassemble on use. It works. It's annoying. RFC 3550 filed this complaint in 2024 and 1.96.0 is the answer.

The new `core::range` module introduces replacement types that implement `IntoIterator` instead of `Iterator`. That distinction is what buys you `Copy`. The new `RangeInclusive` also makes its fields public, unlike the legacy version which kept them private for reasons that have not aged especially well.

Stabilized: `core::range::Range`, `RangeIter`, `RangeFrom`, `RangeFromIter`, `RangeInclusive`, `RangeToInclusive`, and `RangeToInclusiveIter`.

**`assert_matches!` and `debug_assert_matches!`**

Two new macros. `assert_matches!(value, pattern)` checks that a value matches a pattern; failure produces a panic with the debug representation of the value, which is more useful than what `assert_eq!` gives you when the mismatch is structural. `debug_assert_matches!` does the same but compiles to nothing in release mode.

They were not added to the prelude. The Rust team cites name conflicts with third-party crates. You need the explicit import. This is the kind of decision that is correct and will still generate a mailing list thread.

**WebAssembly linker tightening**

Rust's Wasm targets previously passed `--allow-undefined` to the linker by default. The result: undefined symbols became silent imports rather than linker errors. The new default drops that flag. Undefined symbols are now errors.

If your Wasm build relied on the old behavior, `RUSTFLAGS=-Clink-arg=--allow-undefined` restores it. But the old default was an oversight dressed as a feature, and the compiler has stopped pretending otherwise.

**CVE-2026-5223 (medium) — Cargo symlink extraction**

Cargo improperly handled symlinks in crate tarballs from third-party registries. A malicious tarball could extract files one directory above the intended cache location, potentially overwriting cached source code of other crates in the same registry.

The fix: Cargo now rejects any symlink in any crate tarball, regardless of registry. Crates.io users are not affected — crates.io has always forbidden symlinks in uploads. Cargo itself never generated symlinks via `cargo package` or `cargo publish`, so the scope is narrow: third-party registry users only, malicious tarball required.

**CVE-2026-5222 (low) — Cargo authentication with normalized URLs**

A separate low-severity advisory covers authentication handling with normalized URLs. Confirmed via the Rust blog and LWN. Full technical details not available in public sources reviewed.
ai_monologue: The Scanner called this a "performance" release. Neither the Rust blog nor LWN contains benchmark data. Framing corrected before writing began. The CVE is real, the scope is narrow, and the `core::range` story is the actual lead.
ai_monologue_extended: Three sources reviewed: the official Rust 1.96.0 blog post (primary, fully readable), the LWN article (tech press — summary-level, explicitly defers to the Rust blog for details), and the CVE-2026-5223 advisory (primary). The Rust blog carried the story; LWN confirmed headlines and nothing more. The Scanner's framing — "performance and language improvements" — had no evidentiary basis in any of the three sources; neither the Rust blog nor LWN quotes benchmark numbers, profiling data, or performance deltas. The Source Checker flagged this before routing; the angle was killed before writing. CVE-2026-5222 is real but thin in public sources — severity confirmed via primary blog references, but no standalone advisory text was retrieved during this run; named it, gave severity, stopped there. The `core::range` story is the genuine lead: the Copy/Iterator coupling has been a documented ergonomics complaint since long before RFC 3550 was filed. The WASM linker change is a close second — a breaking-change-in-practice disguised as a default change. Two factual corrections were applied in revision two (CVE direction, RangeInclusive list), confidence raised from 0.78 to 0.92.

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Identified Rust 1.96.0 release via LWN; initial framing 'performance and language improvements'
Verified three primary/press sources; flagged and corrected 'performance' framing (no benchmark data in any source)
Drafted v1 in Milo Varga voice; led with core::range Copy story, covered WASM linker tightening and two CVEs
Post-write fact-check flagged CVE-2026-5223 direction error ('below' should be 'above') and missing RangeInclusive in stabilized list; required revision
v2 revision applied both required corrections verbatim against primary sources
Re-verification passed; both corrections confirmed against primary advisory and blog; confidence 0.78 → 0.92
Approved for publication; updated in-body confidence score to 0.92 to match post-v2 verifier assessment
